column8: width:400px"" />Themopylae, 85 mi. northwest of Athens, is the place where in 480 B.C., 300 Spartans and 700 Thespians (temporarily) held off about 300,000 invading Persians led by Xerxes.
column9: The stalled Persians eventually sent a force of men around Leonidas&rsquo
column10: forces, through the mountains to the west of the pass thus encircling the Spartans from in front and behind in the pass.

column1: The Spartans fought valiantly, but eventually all, save two, were killed in battle.&nbsp
column2: The Persians continued their advance on Athens and burned the city.&nbsp
column3: But in pursuit of the Athenians, by land and by sea, they were defeated that year off the island of Salamis.&nbsp
column4: Their final defeat came the next year at the battle of Plataea and the (short) era of peace that followed ushered in the classical period of Greek history.

,4/22/2014 7:20:00AM,False,, 89,Lifting Heavy Objects,
column7: width:177px"" />Over the years I have seen various diagrams illustrating how the ancient Greeks and Romans&mdash
column8: not to mention Herod the Great&mdash
column9: lifted heavy stones into place as they constructed the walls of temples and erected columns.&nbsp
column10: But the first time that I heard about a Lewis Bolt was watching a fascinating DVD put out by The Great Courses entitled Understanding Greek and Roman Technology: From the Catapult to the Pantheon.

column1: I found that the use of Lewis Bolts was actually very common.
column2: So on a recent trip to Turkey and Greece I began to look more carefully at the carvings into column and architectural pieces that might exhibit where Lewis Bolts may have been used.
column3: See now my blog post on this technique.

column1: ,6/13/2014 3:46:00PM,False,, 90,Very Long Roman Bridge in Turkey,
column8: width:400px"" />I have added 8 images of a very long and well&ndash
column9: preserved Roman Bridge that is found in southern Turkey.&nbsp
column10: The preservation and construction details are amazing.

column1: View of the surface of the Roman Road atop the Limyra Bridge.
View looking east along the original surface of the Roman Road that crosses the bridge.
column2: The bridge is 1,181 ft. (360 m.) long!&nbsp
column3: The roadbed is roughly 13 ft. (4 m.) wide and as is evident in the photo there is no evidence of wheel ruts from cars marking the pavement.&nbsp
column4: It thus appears that the bridge was mainly used for foot traffic.&nbsp
column5: Indeed, travel by cart or chariot in Lycia is difficult&mdash

column1: Unfortunately the green houses and orchards are encroaching on the bridge.
column2: Hopefully the local population will leave the bridge intact.

column5: In the area there are over 60 tall isolated geological formations&mdash

column8: Hermits first settled the area in the 5th century A.D. and it was in the 14th century that St. Athanasius established the first monastery on one of these tall sandstone outcrops.&nbsp
column9: Eventually there were 24 monasteries although today there are only 5 of them plus one convent.

, 122,Didyma (Turkey) — A Well Preserved Temple,

The Apollo Temple at Didyma was the site of a world famous oracle of Apollo (compare Delphi! in Greece).
column2: About 300 B.C. Seleucus I Nicator began building the well-preserved Hellenistic Temple that still stands today. For the next 500(!) years it was under construction. It went out of use during the fourth century A.D. as Christianity grew stronger in this region.
